description: Dominion Energy is a regulated U.S. utility company that provides electric and natural gas service across parts of the Mid-Atlantic, Southeast, and Mountain West.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: Dominion Energy is primarily a regulated utility operator, which means much of its business is built around essential electric and natural gas service rather than discretionary consumer technology. Its core role is to generate, transmit, distribute, and sell power, while also operating gas distribution infrastructure in select markets.

For the Free The World registry, Dominion belongs in the infrastructure layer of the economy: the company does not mainly sell digital platforms, but its services shape the practical conditions under which households, businesses, public institutions, and data-intensive systems operate.

overview.title: Product Surface
paragraphs: The company’s most visible products are electric utility service and natural gas service. These are not products in the app-store sense, thankfully, but regulated service bundles involving generation assets, delivery networks, metering, customer billing, outage response, and long-term capital planning.

Dominion’s product analysis should distinguish between customer-facing utility service, grid infrastructure, generation portfolio choices, and any digital customer tools such as account portals, outage maps, energy usage dashboards, or demand-management programs.

overview.title: Registry Relevance
paragraphs: Dominion Energy is relevant to open and decentralized alternatives because utilities sit at the boundary between physical infrastructure and public accountability. Electricity access, pricing transparency, grid resilience, renewable integration, and customer data portability are all areas where locked-down systems can quietly become very expensive forms of dependency.

The first structured sync should focus on mapping Dominion’s regulated service territories, energy mix, customer programs, and digital account infrastructure before trying to compare it to alternatives. In this sector, the alternative is rarely a single replacement company; it is often a mix of public power, cooperatives, distributed generation, community solar, efficiency tools, and open energy data standards.

sourceSuggestions.title: Dominion Energy Official Website
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Primary source for customer-facing electric and gas services, outage tools, billing, and program descriptions.

sourceSuggestions.title: Dominion Energy Investor Relations
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Central source for investor presentations, financial filings, strategy updates, and regulated utility business segmentation.

sourceSuggestions.title: Dominion Energy SEC Filings
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Authoritative filings for business risks, operating segments, regulation, capital spending, and energy portfolio disclosures.

sourceSuggestions.title: Dominion Energy Sustainability and ESG Reports
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Useful for emissions targets, generation mix, environmental claims, and the company’s preferred account of its transition plans.

sourceSuggestions.title: U.S. Energy Information Administration Company and Utility Data
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Independent federal data source for utility generation, sales, customers, plants, fuel mix, and operating statistics.

sourceSuggestions.title: Federal Energy Regulatory Commission
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Relevant for transmission, wholesale market, reliability, and federal regulatory context affecting large electric utilities.

sourceSuggestions.title: CompaniesMarketCap Dominion Energy Market Cap
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Supports registry market-cap context and index-batch provenance, though it should not be treated as an operating source.
